item.title: Have any UK anons faked a job reference before? — 10 replies — 40.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Have any UK anons faked a job reference before?

Open thread • Replies: 10 • Updated: 2026-07-13 17:25 UTC

OP

Have any UK anons faked a job reference before? How did you do it? Should I ask family or friends to pretend to be a former employer? Should I make up a fake business that recently shut down? Will any jobs I apply to accept gmail or hotmail emails as job references?

I was thinking of making up a window cleaning company, a small company of 4 or 5 employees that recently shut down, will reference checkers be suspicious that such a company doesn't have an online presence and can I just claim that we recently shut down and closed down our website as an excuse?

Early replies (first 3)

62471772
>>62470742
Tell them to call me, I will them you're a proper geezer

62471810
just start butchering niggers on the street en masse

62472268
bump

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62472272
Yes. What are they going to do? Chase up old employees of closed down businesses on social media?
You were regional sales manager for Toys R Us, congrats.

62472291
>>62470742
>I was thinking of making up a window cleaning company, a small company of 4 or 5 employees that recently shut down
why are you 'making up' a co?
just use one that went provably titsup at approx the right period, maybe not one as obvious as >>62472272. Try not to use 2 or more bustcos as references - you'll start to look unlucky - and some Doctor Who(ever) as another personal reference, and setup a gmail (DrBlah@gmail.com) in case someone actually contacts this, which they won't. Unless HR are cunts, in which case, you better off out of it.
I dunno what you are applying for, but I'm not aware of many big cos still even asking for references, never mind checking the fucking things. But have a backstory ready just in case they ask.

62472303
>>62472291
I'm applying to entry level customer service/Admin Jobs, minimum wage salary for the UK ~£25,000. These types of Jobs still need references which is retarded.

I'm worried if I use a real company that shut down they'll still have an online presence, former employees who'll be contacted and they'll say that I never worked there. What I was thinking is to give the email of one of my brothers as a reference for the company I made up, and for the interview I'll train really well to answer questions related to the fake company I made up and the role that I supposedly did there.

62472316
>>62472303
>Customer Service
I'd find a plausibly local call centre that went titsup or got taken over, rndm example:
https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cr5804d8l6po
figure out what the format of their co. emails would be - myoldboss@totallytitsupnow.co, you gold. No-one checks this shit ever, not if it's a company name email, and worst case scenario, it just bounces.

62472440
>>62470742
If you're putting a window cleaning company on your CV then the job you're applying for doesn't care about references at all

62475473
bump

62476497
>>62470742
this shit prevented me getting a normal job out of uni
nobody waned me that i needed 5 faggots on call to vouch for me
i had to get a trade instead of jew daycare and while the money is better it does mean actual work
>>62472303
>~£25,000.
it's literally not worth getting out of bed for that but i know it's supposed to be a stepping stone

>>62452288

Don’t
Hire

62474751
even though I'm not a neet after being laid off I feel like I'm in a similar situation.
I have a leg up with my work experience and education but I don't feel like it was worth it which meant that I wasted my youth doing something even worse than just fucking around

62474795
to all the people in this situation who want actual sane advice, here it:

Do something easy. Something that won't demand a superior work ethic or a miserable grind with a manager looking at production numbers watching you.
You're likely gonna hate your life every single morning you wake up and have to work AND you didn't do this when you were 20 years old and it was easy. You can get over the hump and become a "worker" but the mornings are already your enemy, don't stack the odds further against you.

Work behind a register. Ideally at a gas station or some sleepy business. Maybe a long shot for you to get hired, but it's ideal. Some cleaning, some dealing with dickheads, some stocking. You won't be worked to the bone. A grocery/dollar store cashier is worse in most cases.

Inbound call centre. Yes you will hate your job but the work comes at you and your office should be air conditioned. If you have superior (compared to your coworkers) manners and people skills you may even find yourself promoted rapidly. 60 year old grannies get jobs in call centers and end up trainers and managers, you can do it.

Janitor. This one may be a stretch for a 40yo NEET but if you get the right gig, or hop into the right gig after getting a year experience, you may have found a cozy job. Many janitors are not running around like rats and barely have to deal with coworkers or the public. Maybe you end up hired by the local school board in half a year and make "decent" money.


If you really want to try your hand at job where you have a taskmaster watching you try these:

Warehouse worker.
Meatpacker.
Day laborer: Construction, landscaping, whatever.
Factory as a production worker. GL getting hired unless the job is literal hell with high turnover.
Outbound Call Centre

Good luck. If you show up every day for a 1 year or 2 a manager or senior coworker will likely give you a glowing reference. You can even become a real "worker" and have easier mornings in time.

62474821
>>62474795
None of those are hiring.

62474824
>>62474821
Then keep whining here like a bitch. Someone else will take the advice and make a few dollars. If a call centre won't take you, you may as well start picking up cans and bottles.

62474838
>>62474824
Just admit that you're wrong. There are no jobs.

62474844
>>62474795
if you're a man you don't exist. don't go to die in a dead end gas station. karma is real.

62474884
>>62449961
Anon, try asking an LLM (I recommend chat gpt but use whatever) on how to get a job. Explain your entire situation in as much detail as possible, why you haven't worked, depression, you're 40, never had a job, where you live (so it can recommend options) that you're carless.

Explain EVERYTHING. Learning how to utilize LLMs will really help you anyway. I'm getting close to 40 myself and I use LLMs daily (I am a professional with a good job not a NEET but it doesn't matter you need to use LLMs now even if you're employed). Anons ITT are mostly going to dunk on you because you're a huge fucking loser but the best time to turn your life around is right the fuck now. The LLM won't make you feel bad like everyone here is doing too.

Explain your interests to it too and maybe it can try and recommend a job you'll actually excel somewhat in, although beggars can't be choosy at this point.

62474892
>>62474884
the creature making the OP does not want advice. It's doing an act for (you)s
This is actually good advice to someone so defeated they can't find jobs felons on the sex offender registry get after 15 years binned up.
I'm sure a free AI could coach a defeated NEET into one of those jobs.
OP isn't though, just a weird fuck

62475051
>>62474884
I think this is the first time I've seen someone call Ai like chat gpt an "LLM". Well, I guess I've got nothing better to do.

>>62474892
Get a life you fucking loser. Dogshit like you doesn't deserve even the attention I've given you already.

62475932
>>62475051
>Well, I guess I've got nothing better to do.
Repeating myself, but the modern human needs to get comfortable using LLMs to be productive and successful. Avoid falling in to emotional or political traps involving them. View them as a useful tool. A human who refuses to use a calculator would be less productive than a human who does use a calculator.

The same is true for LLMs. Don't blindly trust everything it tells you. Get a feel for how to prompt it to give you the results you want and double check things that you aren't sure about. A lot of people on /biz/ and in general seethe over LLMs, call you a pajeet if you mention them etc, just ignore them. I'm a professional making 6 figs a year in an white collar position, I use LLMs every single day outside of work and inside of work.

As a skill-less NEET who doesn't know how to navigate the world it's the most invaluable tool at your disposal.

62475990
>>62449961
very white european of you

62476030
>>62475932
You put way too much faith in an algorithm poorly masquerading as ai. If it's a tool it should be a learning tool, not a crutch.

62476048
>>62476030
Nowhere did I say it's a crutch, I very clearly said it's a tool. You're starting to sound like you're beyond helping desu. Good luck.

62476501
>>62453688
The fact that you think there is any significant amount of “attacks” in this thread and not genuine criticism shows how far removed you are from the world. It’s going to be a tough couple years for you as you enter the workforce. You’ve received tons of honest advice.

62466346
I'm buying small but keeping plenty of cash on the side. Nobody knows where the bottom is, so position sizing matters more than making the perfect call. Candora's Slippage Protection will help reduce the impact of unexpected execution price changes once it launches.

with just a cpa loicense which you get through some expedited programs but Im not sure.
Look up some Clarey's vids he has a hard on for accounting and has always recommended it.

If you can get in without a degree than you should do that, you can get a degree on the side
through online programs while you are actually working.

If you have to get a degree than again - study online, don't waste time and money going to a brick and mortar
place it aint worth it.

62475259
>>62475211
i went back to school for accounting at 27. it's not a bad job. try to decide ahead of time what type of accounting you want to do: tax or anything else.

tax: if you want to work for yourself, go to a small to medium sized public accounting firm. i wouldn't bother with big 4 or large firms, because you won't be dealing with things that concern you as a future tax preparer for small businesses and individuals.

anything else: try to break into a large public accounting firm. you'll have the most opportunities and the best exit opportunities.

i went big 4 audit very briefly, spent the last 6 years in corporate accounting. kind of wish i had done tax (or law school), but it's not a bad job, like i said.

62475605

62475644
>>62475427
>>62475451
The thing about AI is it is useful, but even in its current iteration it has fundamental flaws that mean for specific jobs it can never do as good as an experienced human. Inexperienced people using AI will do better than if they didn't in many cases, but anybody with decent experience sees the output and sees all of the things wrong with it.
As someone put it, people think AI makes good charts or slides or reports, and then the people who are really good at making charts or slides or reports all say "No that looks bad".

I'm a software engineer and I use a decent amount of AI and had it do great for me, but there's still a number of problems I've thrown at it and it produced total unworkable trash. The thing is, if I was inexperienced I would think it looked good. As someone with 15 years of experience though, I could see its solution would break down rapidly with any other alterations we needed to make.
The experienced human in the loop is still required for anything beyond the absolute basic. As someone who is also in the financial space, AI is absolutely not going to be capable of keeping all the ducks of accounting and finance laws in a row versus an experienced human being.

The whole "return everything free of charge" policies are sure to be part of the reason why everything is more expensive these days.. people exploit it like crazy and of course the losses the retailers get will get baked into the pricing. It isn't like they are doing it from the goodness of their hearts or anything, but for a while I believe that having generous return policies meant more sales, but these days I am sure many are reconsidering the whole thing as it has nurtured traits in a large portion of online shoppers that is no good where they expect to be taken care of even in instances where they have messed up something. The old adage "caveat emptor" means absolutely nothing in today's ecommerce climate.

Here the local Harbor Freight equivalent started lending out tools for weekend jobs free of charge because so many people went in there bought something and then returned it over the weekend when they had done a job with it.

Costco has either two choices, either create their own team of repairmen to offer repairs rather than replacements for big ticket items to limit losses rather than returns or they have to stop the abuse.

Stupid leaders will probably end up limiting what is allowed to be returned and hurt the company more when a defective product is treated like a scam attempt and blown up on the internet

love your attitude op so i've come to bring you knowledge that yall may already know. i'm almost at the 6 figure table.

1. live with your parents, you losers are probably already doing this. with minimal living expenses you can invest more. also allows you to drop the emergency fund which again lets you invest more.

2. be young, youth is an advantage for compounding interest. a huge advantage.

3. get a job, yes a wage slave. obviously a better job will allow you to invest more. it won't be forever. the sooner you hit the rpg grind irl the sooner you can spend the rest of your life eating hot pockets and playing vidia.

4. invest in growth etf's. your crypto is fools gold. yes the stock market can crash but it always bounces back even if it takes years to do it. and you all are young enough to ride out the bad years. once you get old, or have enough then sell your growth etf and get dividend etfs and live the good life.

5. you can't just invest in safe etf's but you have to place them in the right location.

5a) 401k up to employer match, this is the best because its free money for you but the investments are usually not the best. if you have the option get the ROTH 401k not the normal one if the company offers it. for a normal 401k what you put in is tax differed, when you draw from it at 59 1/2 then you pay taxes on it.

5b) ROTH ira is you own personal retirement account. you pay taxes on what you put in but no taxes on what grows inside or when you pull your money out. 59 1/2 also. has a limit on how much you can invest into it each year so max it.

5c) health savings account, hsa. what you contribute can be deducted from your taxes, what grows in it not taxed and when you spend it on a medical expense its not taxed. if you save your bills you can reimburse yourself at a later date once the account grows huge. at 65 you can just withdraw from the account as if it was income.

im just some old fag trying to help my younger brothers.

62474400
>>62470191
>1
mostly agree, but you should never drop the emergency fund
even if you have a great relationship with your parents, anything can happen that changes your living situation (e.g. relationships sour, parental hardship trickles down, new job opportunity requires relocation etc.)
just keep the fund in a money market fund since the (sometimes) higher yield of hysa's often come with restrictions (i.e. daily/monthly transfer limits from the account to your checking)

>2
true even if mostly uncontrollable
staying healthy may prolong life and therefore extend working/investing "lifespan"

>3
true

>4
>growth etfs
>dividend etfs
this is basically sector/style tilt and ill-advised/suboptimal
you should invest in the broad, global market rather than being particular about if a collection of stocks are growth or value or pay dividends
there are a lot of great stocks in all groups and you shouldn't exclude them because of a selection bias
that whole "dividends are income" concept is a trap when you consider that stock price drops by the same amount as the dividend; it's basically like a forced liquidation of your shares

continued in next post

62474455
>>62470191
>>62474400
part 2:

>5
mathematically asset location (which account holds which type of investments [stocks, bonds, cash equivalents etc.]) is optimal but (and this is personal opinion) the deposit limits of different accounts can make it difficult to maintain 100% efficient asset allocation (the specific percentages of your portfolio that are in stocks, bonds etc.)
for example, let's say you have a traditional 401k ($1000 in bonds) and roth ira ($2000 in stocks) that are perfectly balanced to your desired portfolio (2/3 stocks and 1/3 bonds) and you have $1000 to invest
since you weren't sure if a bill was going to come up this year, you didn't invest this money and it is now december
while it's easy to dump the $667 in your roth ira stocks, what are you going to do about the $333 bond investment? since bonds grow slower, it's usually recommended to put them in traditional accounts to mitigate the future tax bill while you stuff your roth with stocks. but you can't just shove it into your 401k since all contributions have to be deducted from your paycheck rather than originating from your bank
in this example $333 is small compared to most paychecks (it's like an 80 hour pay period at $4.16 an hour) and therefore easy to redirect, but the same can't be said if your invest amount is significantly larger than $1000

>5a
mostly agree
i would argue opting for traditional 401k if you live paycheck to paycheck (or slightly above) since it allows you to invest a little more (although pretax) than if you invest after tax (like with a roth) and have a little more net pay than if you just accept tax on your entire gross amount (i imagine this is dependent on your state/local tax amount)

>5b
agree

continued in next post

62474470
>>62470191
>>62474400
>>62474455
part 3:

>5c
mostly true with two caveats, california and new jersey
both states treat them as regular brokerage accounts and therefore tax capital gains (gains made when you sell) and capital distributions (interest, dividends etc.)
i believe most hsa accounts don't create a tax form for you, so you have to manually go through and count what's taxable yourself
the workaround is to hold investments exempt from state tax, which is nothing but us government bonds
for me (personal opinion for rest of post) sinking funds into treasuries which will grow (long term) less than stocks isn't worth it
if you're investing, then you're probably capable of saving and having an emergency fund
that's why i would just chuck would be hsa contributions into a taxable brokerage account
this way, you won't get penalized on taking capital distributions out of the account to pay taxes on said distributions (as opposed to being penalized for withdrawing from hsa or paying the extra tax out of pocket)
yes you lose federal tax advantages, but that's irrelevant if you don't have enough to max out your roth ira each year
instead of having a little bit of money to invest and trying to decide which account to throw it in, you should get your 401k match before maxing roth ira before circling back to max 401k (possibly roth 401k) before worrying about hsa/brokerage
also, even if you don't live in ca or nj, i think those states still tax your hsa if you ever move to those states (like if chasing a new job opportunity) regardless of which state you opened the account in
